---
title: "Background"
---

This site monitors open-access publishing at the [TU9](https://www.tu9.de/) universities of technology, using [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/) and snapshotting the figures over time.
The headline metric is the open-access share of each university's corresponding-author output; a few entity-level indicators are kept as context.
Nothing is scraped work by work — the counts come from OpenAlex's server-side aggregation.

## Which institutions

The tracked institutions are the nine universities of the TU9 alliance — one OpenAlex institution each. Affiliated organisations such as university hospitals and research institutes are **not** tracked as separate institutions; where they belong to a university they enter only through the [consolidated view](#consolidated-view-leiden-ranking) below.
The full list, with OpenAlex and [ROR](https://ror.org/) IDs, is in [`data-raw/institutions.csv`](https://github.com/slub/tu9-openalex/blob/main/data-raw/institutions.csv) and can be edited to change the selection.

## Open access on the corresponding-author lens

The denominator is not a university's whole output but the works whose *corresponding author* is affiliated with it, identified through OpenAlex's [`corresponding_institution_ids`](https://developers.openalex.org/api-reference/works#top-level) filter. This corresponding-author lens aligns with the institutional attribution commonly used in [OpenAPC](https://openapc.net/) and transformative-agreement reporting.

The site reports the following corresponding-author figures, identified by the `ca_` prefix:

- **`ca_works`** — works with a corresponding author at the university;
- **`ca_oa_works`** / **`ca_oa_share`** — how many of those works are open access (`is_oa:true`) and the corresponding share;
- **`ca_doaj_works`** / **`ca_doaj_share`** — how many of those works appeared in a journal listed in [DOAJ](https://doaj.org/), using `primary_location.source.is_in_doaj:true`.

These figures are reported by publication year.
Corresponding-author works and OA share are additionally summarized for the published multi-year period, while the latest complete reference year is highlighted on each institution page.
DOAJ counts and shares remain part of the yearly table.
For the reference year, the site also reports the mutually exclusive OA-status composition: gold, hybrid, green, bronze, diamond, and closed.

All of this comes from OpenAlex's server-side `group_by` aggregation (per-year and per-status counts), so no individual works are downloaded.

Whether this corresponding-author OA share is a reliable enough basis for routine OA monitoring is exactly the question this dataset is meant to help answer.

## Consolidated view (Leiden Ranking)

Whether a university hospital or an affiliated institute counts as part of "the university" is a judgement call.
The [CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition](https://open.leidenranking.com/) makes that judgement explicitly: it links affiliated organisations to a core university as `component` (weight 1, counted fully — e.g. the university hospital), `joint` (fractional), or `associated` (listed but not counted).
The TU9 slice of that mapping is extracted, reproducibly, by `scripts/leiden_affiliations.py` into [`data-raw/leiden_affiliations.csv`](https://github.com/slub/tu9-openalex/blob/main/data-raw/leiden_affiliations.csv) (source: CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition 2025 — Data, [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/records/17471989), CC0; OpenAlex snapshot 08/2025).

So each university appears two ways: as the single OpenAlex institution (identified by its ROR id), and *consolidated* — its OpenAlex id OR-ed with those of its weight-1 `component` affiliates in one `corresponding_institution_ids` filter, so a work still counts once.
The consolidated unit is where, for instance, the [University Hospital Carl Gustav Carus](https://openalex.org/I4210162051) is counted inside TU Dresden and the [Universitätsklinikum Aachen](https://openalex.org/I4210120689) inside RWTH.
Fractional `joint` affiliates are not (yet) included — only full components.
A university without any consolidates to itself, so all nine have a consolidated value and the two views stay comparable across the alliance.

## Multi-institutional hierarchy (OpenAlex/ROR)

A third view groups each university with every institution in *its own* OpenAlex `lineage` — the descendant tree OpenAlex/ROR itself models via `institutions?filter=lineage:<id>`, walked to whatever depth it goes, not just one level. This is deliberately a different judgement from the curated Leiden mapping above: it is OpenAlex/ROR's own automatically-derived organisational hierarchy, not a methodologist's reviewed `component`/`joint`/`associated` classification. Kept as an independent reading to diff against Leiden's curated one, not because it is expected to sit between the single-institution and consolidated figures — it isn't, reliably. As with the consolidated view, it is combined with the corresponding-author lens and `is_xpac:false`, and a university without lineage descendants is reported as itself, so all nine have a value.

Leiden consolidation and OpenAlex/ROR's lineage define genuinely different sets, diverging in both directions at once, not just missing a few edge cases. For TU Dresden: the [University Hospital Carl Gustav Carus](https://openalex.org/I4210162051) is a Leiden `component` but *not* in TU Dresden's OpenAlex lineage (OpenAlex links it as `related`, not `child`) — so consolidated counts it and hierarchy does not. Conversely, roughly twenty small OpenAlex-modelled research centres (many created in one apparent batch, e.g. under the DRESDEN-concept umbrella) sit in TU Dresden's lineage without being a Leiden `component` at all — so hierarchy counts them and consolidated does not. Checked across all nine TU9 universities, the two member sets produce a hierarchy figure that exceeds the Leiden-consolidated figure for six of them and falls short for three — there is no reliable ordering between the two, only the guarantee (checked in `scripts/validate.R`) that hierarchy, like consolidated, can only add works relative to the single-institution view.

Because it is fetched live rather than drawn from a versioned, reviewed mapping, this view's membership can also shift between snapshots for reasons unrelated to publishing activity — for instance when a batch of ROR edits (an affiliation-editor submission, a periodic ROR release) is later reflected in OpenAlex's own institution graph. The published `n_members` count is carried specifically so such a shift is visible rather than silently absorbed into the figures.

## Core-source-filtered views (CWTS Core sources)

Two further views keep the same Leiden-consolidated member set — the university's OpenAlex institution id OR-ed with its weight-1 `component` affiliates — but additionally restrict works to sources on the CWTS Core sources allow-list.
They are two distinct readings of that allow-list, computed as separate queries and never combined:

- **Primary Core venue** (`primary_location.source.is_core:true`) — a work qualifies only when its *primary* venue is a Core source.
- **Any Core location** (`locations.source.is_core:true`) — a work qualifies when *at least one* of its recorded locations is a Core source.

Each is combined with the corresponding-author lens (`corresponding_institution_ids:<university-and-component-ids>`) and the usual XPAC exclusion (`is_xpac:false`); the numerator additionally requires `is_oa:true`.
Because the primary location is one of the locations, the primary-venue set is contained in the any-location set, which is in turn contained in the unconstrained consolidated view: primary-venue Core ≤ any-location Core ≤ consolidated, for both the works count and its open-access subset.

Both views are meaningful even for universities without Leiden components, because the source filter itself narrows the publication universe.
Both are populated for all nine TU9 universities.

Important caveat: both are **source-only Core filters**, not a reproduction of the official Leiden Ranking `core publication` set.
The official CWTS definition of a core publication applies additional publication-level criteria, including language, work type, source type, references, affiliation availability and retraction status.
The Zenodo records below provide the versioned source list and methodology; the live OpenAlex `is_core` flag may evolve after the August 2025 snapshot.

### Provenance

- "Core sources and core publications in OpenAlex", CWTS, 2025. DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17200868>. Standalone source list (`source.tsv`) including `source_id`, `source`, `source_type`, `issn_l`, `is_core_source`, `n_works` and `n_core_works`, based on the August 2025 OpenAlex snapshot.
- "A methodology for identifying core sources and core publications in OpenAlex", CWTS, 2025. DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17200830>. Methodology report plus the supplementary source spreadsheet (`Supplementary material - Sources.ods`).
- CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition 2025 — Data. DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17471989>. The `pub.tsv` table includes an `is_core_pub` publication-level field, but the standalone, auditable source list is the separate Core-sources dataset above.

## Alliance-level deduplication

The landing page's "Coverage of the alliance" table does not sum the nine per-institution figures above. Summing them can double-count a work: if its corresponding-author affiliations resolve to more than one TU9 member (through multiple affiliations, or through genuine cross-institution collaboration), that work is counted once in each member's own figure. For each of the five readings, the site instead runs one further `group_by` query with every member university's relevant OpenAlex id — across all nine universities, deduplicated — OR-ed into a single `corresponding_institution_ids` filter, so such a work is counted once.

The table reports, for the published multi-year period:

- **Distinct CA works** — *U*, the works count from that one deduplicated query;
- **CA OA share** — *U*<sub>OA</sub> / *U*, the direct ratio of open-access works to all works in that same query — not a median, average, or weighted average of the nine institutional shares.

The five queries are the same reasoning already used for the per-institution single/hierarchy/consolidated/Core views, just run once more over the union of all nine universities' ids instead of one at a time — costing ten additional `group_by` requests (one denominator/numerator pair per view), not one request per member institution. The underlying yearly rows are published in `data/alliance_ca_oa_by_year.csv`, keyed by `(view, year)`; like the other grouped-response products, it is a latest-snapshot-only file with no historical series and no per-institution slice, because a deduplicated alliance-wide count has no single institution to slice it by.

### Diagnostic: cross-institutional overlap

Beside *U*, `meta.json`'s alliance entries also carry *S* (`sum_ca_works`), its OA counterpart (`sum_ca_oa_works`), and `overlap_share` = (*S* − *U*) / *S*, where *S* is the sum of the nine institutional CA-work counts already shown on the [Institutions](institutions/index.qmd) page for that reading. This is the share of that sum removed by deduplication; zero means no overlap. It is validated (`scripts/validate.R` checks `overlap_share` equals `(S − U) / S` and falls in `[0, 1]`) and published for those who want to inspect it.

The current overlap values are only about 0.8–1.0%, meaning that deduplication changes the alliance totals very little. This is unsurprising: a work contributes to the overlap only when its corresponding-author affiliations match more than one TU9 member. Most collaborative publications do not meet that narrow condition. The measure is therefore retained in `meta.json` as a deduplication diagnostic rather than shown as a headline figure. It should not be interpreted as a cooperation rate: the overlap may result either from multiple corresponding authors at different member institutions or from a single corresponding author listing multiple affiliations.

## Context metrics

Beside the OA figures, each snapshot keeps entity-level context: output counts under XPAC-excluded, XPAC-inclusive, and lineage-inclusive lenses, and citation indicators (cited-by count, h-index, i10-index, 2-year mean citedness). These are archived in `metrics.csv` and the raw per-snapshot entity JSON (`data/snapshots/<slug>/<date>.json`) but are **not** shown on institution pages: they use an any-author lens rather than the corresponding-author one this site is built around, mix XPAC-inclusive and XPAC-excluded figures depending on the field, and — being precomputed OpenAlex entity aggregates rather than live queries — lag the live API for a year still filling up. See [Downloads](downloads.qmd) for the files. Every *displayed* query on this site, including the hierarchy view above, still applies `is_xpac:false` explicitly rather than relying on OpenAlex's current default.

## How it is assembled

1. For every university in `data-raw/institutions.csv`, the pipeline looks up its OpenAlex/ROR `lineage` descendants (an [institutions](https://developers.openalex.org/api-reference/institutions) search, cursor-paginated) and runs a handful of OpenAlex [works](https://developers.openalex.org/api-reference/works) `group_by` queries — corresponding-author works by year and OA status, total works by year, the OpenAlex/ROR hierarchy corresponding-author view, the Leiden-consolidated corresponding-author view, and that consolidated view restricted to CWTS Core sources under both readings (`primary_location.source.is_core:true` and `locations.source.is_core:true`) — and reads the institution entity for the citation indicators, using the polite pool and, where configured, a free OpenAlex API key. Once every university has been fetched, five further deduplicated `group_by` queries run over the UNION of member ids across all nine universities, one per reading (see [Alliance-level deduplication](#alliance-level-deduplication)) — ten additional requests in total.
2. Everything is fetched into memory first and only then validated: the exact configured institution set, every required product per university, a single consistent snapshot date, and the arithmetic behind each OA share. A coverage guard rail additionally aborts if the total works count drops sharply against the previous snapshot. If this pre-write validation fails, nothing is written.
3. Only after that are the files written: a dated row per university is appended to `data/metrics.csv` (re-running on the same day replaces that day's rows rather than duplicating them), the publication-year and OA views are rewritten for the latest snapshot, and `meta.json` is regenerated. Products that no longer apply are removed rather than left behind as stale files. The stored products are then read back and validated again before they are committed or deployed.

## Caveats

OpenAlex coverage and attribution evolve continuously — affiliation strings are disambiguated over time, and works are added or reassigned.
A change between two snapshots therefore reflects both real publishing activity and improvements in OpenAlex's own data.
Figures are best read as a consistent, reproducible series rather than as an authoritative count.
